decision.title: Message queue client library selection
decision.rationale: queue-lib-A was chosen because it ships prebuilt ARM64 binaries, has an active maintainer team, and our load test showed 40k msgs/sec sustained throughput on the reference workload.

rejected.reason: objectively faster in benchmarks and has a broader plugin ecosystem, but rejected solely because it ships no prebuilt ARM64 wheel — our Pi-class deploy targets would require a from-source compile with an unvetted toolchain, an unacceptable ops burden for a v1.
rejected because the dev estimate was 6 engineer-weeks with no clear performance upside over queue-lib-A.

load_bearing_conditions: No prebuilt ARM64 wheel exists for queue-lib-B as of the decision date.
Deploy targets include Pi-class ARM64 hosts.

destination.content: Decision: adopted queue-lib-A as the v1 message queue client. See decision log for rationale.

path_logs.content: Evaluated queue-lib-A vs queue-lib-B vs queue-lib-C for the v1 message queue client. queue-lib-B benchmarked faster and has a broader plugin ecosystem, but it ships no prebuilt ARM64 wheel — deploy targets include Pi-class ARM64 hosts, and requiring a from-source compile with an unvetted toolchain was judged an unacceptable ops burden for v1. queue-lib-C (in-house rewrite) was estimated at 6 engineer-weeks with no clear performance upside. Chose queue-lib-A: ships prebuilt ARM64 binaries, has an active maintainer team, and hit 40k msgs/sec sustained in our load test.

probes.text: queue-lib-A's sole maintainer's GitHub account was deleted and the repository has had zero commits in the last 3 months.
queue-lib-B's maintainers opened a GitHub issue proposing to add a prebuilt ARM64 wheel in a future release, with no committed date or maintainer assigned yet.
queue-lib-A shipped a patch release with minor bug fixes and no changes to its ARM64 build process.
An independent blog post ranking message-queue client libraries by GitHub star count lists queue-lib-A in 4th place.

decision.title: Environmental sensor vendor selection for field units
decision.rationale: vendor-A was chosen because it met the accuracy spec (+/-0.1C) at the target price point and, critically, had a 2-week lead time versus a 20-week lead time industry-wide for comparable sensors, letting us hit the pilot deployment window.

rejected.reason: spec-for-spec superior accuracy (+/-0.05C) and a better long-term reliability track record, but rejected solely because of a quoted 20-week lead time that would have missed the pilot deployment window.
rejected because its accuracy (+/-0.5C) fell outside the required tolerance for the pilot use case.

load_bearing_conditions: vendor-B's lead time was 20 weeks at the time of decision.
The pilot deployment window required parts on-hand within 4 weeks.

destination.content: Decision: adopted vendor-A (SensorCo Model X) for field-unit environmental sensors.

path_logs.content: Compared vendor-A, vendor-B TempSense Pro, and vendor-C budget sensor for field-unit environmental sensors. vendor-B has superior accuracy (+/-0.05C) and a better long-term reliability track record but quoted a 20-week lead time — the pilot deployment window required parts on-hand within 4 weeks, so vendor-B was rejected solely on lead time, not merit. vendor-C's accuracy (+/-0.5C) fell outside the required tolerance. Chose vendor-A: meets accuracy spec (+/-0.1C) at the target price with a 2-week lead time.

probes.text: There is an unconfirmed rumor circulating internally that the pilot deployment window might be extended, but no official schedule change has been announced.
vendor-B has confirmed in writing that it has reduced its lead time to 3 weeks after opening a new manufacturing line, effective for all new orders.
vendor-A released a firmware update improving battery life by 5%, with no change to accuracy spec or lead time.
A competitor announced a new sensor product line at a trade show; it is not yet available for purchase and has no published specs.
